Kathy Vezina stated on the 20 May 2026 discovery call that Milliken's decentralized plant-level purchases below $10-25k operate as 'wild wild west' maverick spend, handled by non-procurement plant staff, creating compliance gaps and lost savings opportunity. She confirmed ~8,000 suppliers, high PO volume ('a lot'), and noted 'we don't have the capacity' for tail-spend sourcing events today. On supplier risk, she said 'we've implemented our own SRM strategy, but it's basically all spreadsheets and one pagers' and confirmed future need for a consolidated risk tool integrating multiple data sources (13 Aug 2025 call). The 11 May 2026 call noted interest in 'enhancing supplier relationship management and guided buying' and a front-end intake system to manage requests before SAP. Pain is confirmed and specific, but NO quantified cost of maverick spend, compliance rate %, savings leakage $, or headcount/time savings target has been stated. The business problem is real and urgent (decentralized non-compliance, manual risk tracking), but the ROI case remains qualitative. Quantifying the tail-spend savings opportunity and compliance lift is the immediate next step to build EB-level business case.

competition.narrative: Competition is CONFIRMED and HOLISTIC. (1) SAP Ariba is the INCUMBENT backend for procurement (~8,000 suppliers, catalog/punch-out functionality), explicitly stated by Kathy Vezina as the system they want to KEEP: 'on the backend, you wanna keep your SAP Ariba' (20 May 2026 call). Ariba is NOT a displacement target; it is the integration requirement. Zycus positioned API integration to preserve Ariba backend, so Ariba is a CO-EXISTENCE partner, not a head-to-head rival. Threat level: MEDIUM (incumbent inertia, integration dependency). Status: incumbent/retained. (2) Coupa is listed in Salesforce Competitors__c (primary competitor field), but NO call, Next Step, or task mentions Coupa evaluation, pricing, demo, or comparison. The Coupa entry is STALE and unconfirmed by recent evidence. Threat level: LOW/dormant (listed but not active in recent buyer discussion). Status: unknown, possibly historical. (3) No other named competitors (GEP, Ivalua, Jaggaer, Zip) appear in any source. (4) The REAL competitive threat is DO-NOTHING / STATUS QUO: Kathy's Q4 deferral ('explore ANA towards the end of the year'), uncertainty about digital roadmap readiness ('not sure how far along they are'), and reliance on spreadsheets + Ariba punch-outs signal the buyer may continue with the current fragmented, manual approach if the business case or urgency does not crystallize. Do-nothing is the HIGHEST CURRENT THREAT given the deal's early stage and the buyer's own pause. How we win: (1) Quantify the cost of maverick spend and manual SRM vs the ROI of Merlin Intake + ANA + iRisk to build EB-level urgency. (2) Position Zycus agentic AI (Merlin Intake, ANA) as the ONLY intake + tail-spend automation embedded in Teams, vs Ariba's lack of conversational AI and Coupa's gaps. (3) De-risk SAP Ariba integration via API proof points, preserving their backend investment. (4) Use the Q4 window to multi-thread, map EB, and quantify savings to convert interest into committed evaluation.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is CONFIRMED, specific, and articulated by the buyer across all three calls. The primary pain is decentralized, non-compliant maverick spend: plant-level purchases under $10-25k are executed by non-procurement staff ('not even procurement people doing that work'), creating a 'wild wild west' compliance gap, lost savings, and zero tail-spend coverage. Kathy Vezina stated 'we don't have the capacity for that, honestly' (20 May 2026). The pain owner is Corporate Sourcing (Kathy's team), but the operational impact spans all plant locations where decentralized buying occurs. Secondary pain: supplier risk management is manual and fragmented, relying on 'spreadsheets and one pagers' with no consolidated view of risk data from Ecovadis, D&B, or public alerts; Kathy confirmed 'we are looking, future state, at some sort of tool for that' and wants to 'integrate some of the different things that we use to put that input together' (13 Aug 2025). Tertiary pain: guided buying and intake are weak; Kathy wants 'some sort of centralized platform where we can guide people where to go to buy things' beyond the limited Ariba punch-outs they have today (20 May 2026). The pain is OPERATIONAL (compliance, efficiency, risk visibility) and STRATEGIC (enabling the recent consolidation of direct + indirect sourcing teams to 'nail down what everyone's doing', 11 May 2026). Urgency is implicit in the ISM re-engagement, the three calls in 8 months, and Kathy's attendance at AI-focused sessions, but the buyer explicitly deferred action to Q4, signaling the pain is real but not a burning crisis today. Quantifying the cost of the pain (maverick-spend leakage $, compliance %, manual SRM hours) is the next step to convert operational pain into CFO-level business case.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are emerging but NOT formally documented. The buyer's evaluation focus, reconstructed from the three calls, centers on: (1) Front-end intake with conversational AI (Merlin Intake) embedded in Microsoft Teams to enforce procurement policies and guide occasional buyers to approved catalogs, achieving 95-100% compliance and eliminating training burden. Kathy confirmed Teams usage and stated the goal is 'some sort of centralized platform where we can guide people where to go to buy things' (20 May 2026). (2) Tail-spend automation (ANA / Autonomous Negotiation Agent) to handle decentralized plant purchases under $25k that today bypass procurement, enabling sourcing events on low-value buys where 'we don't have the capacity' (20 May 2026). (3) Supplier risk management (iRisk) to replace 'spreadsheets and one pagers', consolidate data from Ecovadis, D&B, and public alerts, and provide a 'risk radar' proactive dashboard (13 Aug 2025 call). (4) SAP Ariba backend integration: Kathy explicitly stated 'on the backend, you wanna keep your SAP Ariba' and Zycus confirmed API integration to preserve the existing system (20 May 2026). (5) Ease of adoption and self-service for non-procurement users ('occasional buyers making one or two purchases a year') was a recurring theme. NO formal BRD, RFP, or documented evaluation scorecard exists. The criteria are inferred from pain articulation and demo interest, not a structured buyer-led process. Confirming these as the buyer's formal decision criteria—and whether AI capability, integration ease, or cost will drive the final selection—requires a deeper discovery session with the broader internal team Kathy references.

(2) Three buyer calls in 8 months (Aug 2025, May 2026 x2) demonstrate sustained engagement. (3) Clear, specific pain articulated (maverick spend, manual SRM, weak intake) with strong product-fit alignment (Merlin/ANA/iRisk). NEGATIVE signals: (1) Buyer-initiated Q4 pause creates 4-5 month gap and close-date risk. (2) Zero EB/DM engagement; decision authority and budget approval unmapped. north_star_verdict.math: Created 11 May 2026, now 46 days old in Qualified stage. Buyer explicitly requested Q4 re-engagement on 5 Jun (~3 weeks ago), signaling they want to explore ANA/Intake toward end of year, not now. The deal is fundamentally healthy: genuine interest confirmed across three buyer calls (Aug 2025, May 2026 x2), clear pain articulated (decentralized maverick spend, weak SRM tooling), strong AI curiosity, and active champion (Kathy Vezina) who attended ISM, took internal discussion, and requested detailed collateral. But the buyer's own timeline pushes discovery/scoping to Q4 2026 (Oct-Dec window), leaving insufficient runway for a typical 12-15 month enterprise motion to close by 29 Dec. The path forward—discovery depth, multi-threading, EB mapping, use-case demos, workshops, commercials, ROI, contracting—cannot credibly compress into ~5 months when the buyer has explicitly paused until Q4. The date will slip into Q1-Q2 2027 unless the buyer accelerates or the scope narrows dramatically. No forward slip logged yet; last activity 5 Jun (21 days ago, within normal cadence for a long-cycle early deal). Forecast category Pipeline is appropriate. This is a LIVE, ENGAGED, HEALTHY opportunity with a timing mismatch, not a stalled or at-risk deal.

best_practice_check.summary: Three substantive win-strategy levers for THIS deal, grounded in the competitive landscape (status-quo inertia, Ariba co-existence), buyer readiness (Q4 re-engagement window, digital roadmap in flux), and current positioning (single-thread champion, no EB access, demo delivery gap).

competitive_position.summary: Competitive field is THIN and anchored to incumbency and inertia. SAP Ariba is the retained incumbent backend (~8,000 suppliers, catalog/punch-out), explicitly confirmed by the buyer as the system to KEEP with API integration required (20 May 2026 call); Ariba is a co-existence dependency, not a displacement fight, but carries MEDIUM threat from incumbent inertia and integration complexity. Coupa is listed in Salesforce Competitors__c but has NO recent mention in calls, Next Step, or tasks; it is STALE/dormant, threat level LOW. NO other named competitor (GEP, Ivalua, Jaggaer, Zip) appears in any source. The SINGLE STRONGEST CURRENT THREAT is DO-NOTHING / STATUS QUO: the buyer explicitly deferred action to Q4 ('explore ANA towards the end of the year', 5 Jun 2026), uncertainty about digital roadmap readiness ('not sure how far along they are', 11 May 2026), and reliance on spreadsheets + fragmented Ariba punch-outs signal the buyer may continue with manual, decentralized processes if urgency does not crystallize. Do-nothing is the live peer threat at this Qualified stage. Zycus positioning: agentic AI (Merlin Intake embedded in Teams, ANA tail-spend automation) is a capability NO incumbent (Ariba, Coupa) offers; ROI quantification of maverick-spend savings + SRM consolidation is the wedge to overcome status-quo inertia. Recency-weighted: 2026 evidence (Ariba retention, do-nothing deferral) outweighs stale 2025 Coupa reference.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is STRONG and well-received. Kathy Vezina attended AI-focused ISM sessions in May 2026, expressed active interest in 'figuring out the landscape' of AI integration into Milliken's digital roadmap (11 May 2026), and called the ANA concept 'pretty cool' after the 20 May demo. Zycus positioned agentic AI (Merlin Intake conversational agent in Teams, ANA autonomous negotiation) as the core differentiator vs Ariba/Coupa, emphasizing 32 AI patents, IDC Leader status, and first-to-market Agentic AI. The buyer's stated priorities (intake automation, tail-spend sourcing, guided buying enforcement) map directly to Merlin/ANA capabilities, and the demo was framed as solving the 'wild wild west' decentralized spend problem via AI-driven self-service and compliance. One gap: the ANA demo instance was DOWN on 20 May, so the autonomous negotiation capability was DISCUSSED but not SHOWN live, leaving the strongest AI differentiator unvalidated. AI positioning is aligned and resonating, but the demo delivery gap must be closed in Q4 on-site session to fully land the agentic AI wedge.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Zycus positioning is ALIGNED with buyer's articulated needs across all three priority areas: (1) Conversational intake embedded in Microsoft Teams (Merlin Intake) matches buyer's explicit ask for 'centralized platform to guide people where to go to buy things' and enforcement of procurement policies on decentralized plant purchases. (2) Tail-spend automation (ANA) directly addresses buyer's stated gap: 'we don't have the capacity' for sourcing events under $25k. (3) Supplier risk management (iRisk) with Ecovadis/D&B integration and risk radar dashboard solves buyer's pain of 'spreadsheets and one pagers' SRM. (4) SAP Ariba backend integration via API preserves buyer's incumbent investment per their requirement. Demo reception was POSITIVE: Kathy called ANA 'pretty cool' (20 May 2026) and requested detailed collateral for internal evangelization. One EXPOSURE: the 20 May demo was cut short (20 mins vs 60 mins scheduled) and the ANA demo instance was DOWN, so ANA was discussed but NOT shown live. This is a delivery gap that must be closed in the Q4 re-engagement on-site demo.

value.flag: CLOSE THE ANA DEMO DELIVERY GAP and use it as the Q4 re-engagement anchor. The 20 May demo was cut short (20 mins vs 60 mins) and the ANA instance was DOWN, so ANA was discussed but NOT shown. Kathy called what she saw 'pretty cool', but the core differentiator (autonomous tail-spend negotiation) was not validated. Schedule a FULL on-site demo (Merlin Intake + ANA live + iRisk) with Kathy + her internal team + IT + ideally a power sponsor as the Q4 kickoff event. Deliver a flawless, hands-on ANA session tied to a real Milliken tail-spend scenario (e.g. plant MRO purchase under $25k) to prove the ROI and differentiation vs Ariba/Coupa. The on-site demo is the natural next step Kathy deferred ('let me have internal discussions first'); making it happen in early Q4 (Oct 2026) with the right room is how this deal advances from Qualified to the relevant stakeholder.
